items.question: State the batch gradient descent update rule and define each symbol.
items.answer: θ ← θ − η∇L(θ): θ is the parameter vector, η > 0 is the learning rate, and ∇L(θ) is the gradient of the average loss over the full training set.

items.question: How does batch gradient descent differ from mini-batch SGD?
items.answer: Both use θ ← θ − η∇L(θ). Batch GD computes ∇L over the entire dataset once per epoch (one update). Mini-batch SGD estimates ∇L from subsets, performing many noisier updates per epoch at lower cost.

items.question: Why can batch GD fail to reach a global minimum on deep networks?
items.answer: Loss surfaces are non-convex with many local minima, saddles and plateaus. GD is a local first-order method—it follows −∇L where it currently stands, so initialization and surface geometry determine where it stops.

items.question: Walk through one epoch of batch GD in PyTorch for a linear model.
items.answer: Forward on all data → compute scalar loss → `loss.backward()` for ∇L → `optimizer.step()` applies θ ← θ − η∇L. Call `zero_grad()` before backward each epoch to avoid accumulating stale gradients.
